reasoning:
The 10-year Treasury yield recently experienced a sharp increase, jumping from 4.26% on May 7 (the date of the FOMC meeting where rates were held steady) to 4.37% on May 9. This upward momentum is driven by inflation concerns, a record $140B trade deficit due to tariff front-running (which bolsters inflation expectations), and the Fed holding rates steady. The primary quantitative anchor provided by the FRED tool indicates a 58% probability of the yield exceeding the 4.37% threshold over the short term (incorporating linear trend and prior year seasonality). 

For the very near-term resolution dates (May 18, June 10), this sticky inflation narrative, recent upward momentum, and seasonal patterns suggest a higher likelihood that yields will remain above the May 11 base value (which will be based on the May 9 close of 4.37%). Hence, our probability estimate is somewhat elevated for the near term (0.59 to 0.56).

However, looking further out into late summer and autumn (August 9, November 7), the macroeconomic consensus—reflected in CME FedWatch probabilities and broad economic forecasts (e.g., CBO estimating an average 4.1% for 2025)—projects 2 to 3 rate cuts in the second half of 2025. These anticipated cuts will likely exert downward pressure on yields, offsetting the current inflation premium. Consequently, the probability of the 10-year yield remaining above the high 4.37% mark decreases, reverting closely toward 50%. In alignment with the directive that uncertainty should strictly increase with the forecast horizon, the model's probabilities transition smoothly from the confident near-term trend toward a highly uncertain ~0.5 for the distant dates.
